content.full_text:
RAVI MEHTA
CHMRMAN
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES
COMMISSION
July 17,
1995
Richard D.
Jones
Law Offices of Richard D.
Jones
Brea Landmark Bank Building
390 North Brea Boulevard,
Suite A
Brea,
CA
92621
Re:
Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No.
1-95-209
Dear Mr.
Jones:
This is in response to your request for advice concerning the
conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act
1
(the
“Act”)
and whether the Act prohibits a councilmember in the City
of Whittier from participating in decisions affecting a waste
hauler company.
Since you have not named the specific official on
whose behalf the advice is requested,
we are treating your request
as one for informal assistance.
2
QUESTION
Does the Act prohibit a councilmember in the City of Whittier
from participating in_decisions affecting a waste hauler company
which has-expended in excess of $10,000 in the-past year with the
councilmember ‘ s business.
CONCLUSION
Absent an exception,
(as discussed below),
a councilmember
who owns a retail business in the jurisdiction must disqualify-
himself from participating in decisions which have a material
financial effect on customers of his business.
1
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Conunission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations,
Section
18000—18995.
All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
2
Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c) (3).)
428 J STREET, SUITE 800, SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814
(916) 322-5660


Our File No.
1-95—209
Page
2
FACTS
A councilmember in the City of Whittier is the sole owner of
a trophy and award promotion business, which includes the
designing and production of speciality shirts and hats for
businesses.
These items typically identify the business name and
logo on a shirt or hat.
This business is operated as a retail
store.
A waste hauler company has expended in excess of $10,000 in
the past year with the councilmember’s business.
The waste hauler
provides services to the City of Whittier and has a contract
coming before the city council for approval.
ANALYSIS
The Political Reform Act was enacted by the people of the
State of California by initiative in 1974.
The purpose for the
disclosure and disqualification provisions of the Act is to ensure
that public officials, whether elected or appointed,
perform their
duties in an impartial manner,
free from bias caused by their own
financial interests or the financial interests of persons who have
supported them.
(Section 81001(b).)
You inquire on behalf of a
councilmember for the City of Whittier who is a public official.
(Section 82048.)
Accordingly,
he is subject to the disclosure and
disqualification provisions of the Act.
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making,
participating in making, or otherwise using his or he official
position to influence a governmental decision in which the
official has a financial interest.
Section 87103 specifies that a
public official has a financial interest in a decision if it is
reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial effect, distinguishable from the effect on the public
generally,
on the official or a memberof his or •her i!nmediat&
family or on:
(a)
Any business entity in which the public
official has a direct or indirect investment worth
one thousand dollars
($1,000)
or more.
(c)
Any source of income other than gifts and
other than loans by a commercial lending institu
tion in the regular course of business on terms
available to the public without regard to official
status,
aggregating two hundred fifty dollars
($250)
or more in value provided to, received by or
promised to the public official within 12 months
prior to the time when the decision is made.


Our File No.
1-95-209
Page
3
(d)
Any business entity in which the public
official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management.
***
For purposes of this section, indirect
investment or interest means any investment or
interest owned by the spouse or dependent child of
a public official,
by an agent on behalf of a
public official, or by a business entity or trust
in which the official, the official’s agents,
spouse,
and dependent children own directly,
indirectly, or beneficially a 10-percent interest
or greater.
Section 87103(a),
(c)
and (d).
For purposes of our discussion we assume that the
councilmember’s interest in his business is worth $1,000 or more
and conclude that customers of his trophy and award business are
sources of income to him based on his pro rata share of the gross
receipts of the business.
(Section 82030(a).)
Thus,
as a general
rule,
any person or business that has been a source of income of
$250 or more to the counilmember or to his business within 12
months prior to a decision is a potentially disqualifying
financial interest as defined in Section 87103.
Consequently, the
councilmember may not participate in any decision if it is
reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial effect on that person or business which is
distinguishable from the effect on the public generally.
Section87l03.5 provides an exception to this general rule.
If retaiL customers of a business entity constitute a significant
segment of the public generally,
and if the amount of income
received from a particular customer is not distinguishable from
the amount of income received from other retail customers, that
customer is not a source of income for purposes of Section
87103(c).
-
-
-
-
The first part of the test which needs to be met is that the
customers of a retail business entity constitute a significant
segment of the public generally.
That would occur if either of
the following applies:


Our File No.
1-95-209
Page
4
(1)
The retail customers of the business
entity during the preceding 12 months are suf
ficient in number to equal 10—percent or more of
the population or households of the jurisdiction;
or
(2)
The retail customers of the business
entity during the preceding 12 months number at
least ten thousand.
Regulation 18703.5(a) (1)
and
(2),
copy enclosed.
The second part of the test which would need to be met is that
the amount of income received by a business entity from a retail
customer is not distinguishable from the amount of income received
from its other retail customers if the amount spent by the
customer in question during the preceding 12 months is less than
one—tenth of one percent of the gross sales of the retail business
entity for the preceding fiscal year.
(Regulation 18703.5(b).)
If both of these requirements are met, the customer is not
considered a source of income.
If the exception does not apply, the councilmember will be
required to disqualify himself from decisions materially affecting
the source of income.
Regulation 18702 sets forth the guidelines
for determining whether an official’s economic interest in a
decision is “materially” affected as required by Section 87103.
If the official’s financial interest is directly involved in the
decision, then Regulation 18702.1 applies to determine
materiality.
On the other hand, if the official’s financial
interest is indirectly affected by the decision,
then Regulations
18702.2 to 18702.6 would apply to determine whether the effect of
the decision. is material.
When customers of the -councilmember’s trophyand award
—
business are directly involved in a decision before the city
council, the councilmember must disqualify himself from
participating in the decision.
3
(Regulation 18702.1(a) (1).)
Under your facts, the decision concerns the waste hauler’s
contract with the city, thus the waste hauler company is directly
involved in the decision.
-
A person or business entity is directly involved in a decision
before an official’s agency when that person or entity, either
personally or by an agent:
(1)
Initiates the proceeding in which
the decision will be made by filing an application,
claim, appeal,
or similar request or;
(2)
Is a named party in,
or is the subject
of, the proceeding concerning the decision before the official or
the official’s agency.
(3)
A person or business entity is the
subject of a proceeding if a decision involves the issuance,
renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit,
or
other entitlement to,
or contract with, the subject person or
business entity.
(Regulation 18702.1(b).)


Our File No.
1-95-209
Page
5
Should you have any further questions regarding this matter,
do not hesitate to call me at
(916)
322-5660.
-
Sincerely,
Steven G.
Churchwell
General Counsel
Lynda Doherty
Political Reform Consultant
Legal Division
ld
Enclosures
